summarization.a: The  chart illustrates the relationship between deposition temperature and minimum purge time for two reagents: metal amide and water. The required purge times generally decrease with increasing temperature. Water generally requires longer purging times than metal amide.

data_extraction.a:
| Deposition Temperature (°C) | Water: Minimum Purge Time (sec) | Metal amide: Minimum Purge Time (sec) |
|---|---|---|
| 50 | 600 | 300 |
| 100 | 300 | 122 |
| 150 | 93 | 31 |
| 200 | 15 | 7 |
| 250 | 7 | 7 |

a.question: Which species generally requires longer purge times?
Do the curves diverge or converge at higher temperatures?  What does it tell about the difference in physisorption strength of water and the metal amide at higher temperatures?
a.answer: The curves converge at higher temperatures. This trend occurs because increasing thermal energy significantly accelerates the desorption rate of physisorbed molecules. Consequently, at high temperatures, the difference in physisorption strength between water and the metal amide becomes negligible, and both can be purged almost instantly.

a.question: At lower temperatures, which reagent is physisorbed more strongly, based on the required purging times?
a.answer: Water is physisorbed more strongly (requires longer purging).
